source: Heller House — SpaceX CFO Bret Johnsen interview — financial mechanics, Starship timeline, orbital compute, AI business
summary: SpaceX's capital allocation is sequenced so that Starlink V3 cash flows fund orbital compute capex — the business model is self-financing at the critical inflection point.
summary_extended: Baker observes, and SpaceX CFO Bret Johnsen confirms, that SpaceX has deliberately sequenced its capital allocation so that Starlink V3 cash flows — enabled by Starship's heavy-lift capability — fund the capital expenditure required for orbital compute infrastructure. The thesis holds that Starlink V3 also unlocks direct-to-cell service at precisely the moment orbital compute requires heavy capital deployment, making the business model largely self-financing at a critical inflection point. Baker frames this as intentional 'Elon-level' capital planning that materially reduces external financing risk for what could be a transformative new compute paradigm in orbit. No specific cash flow figures were provided, but both Baker and Johnsen affirmed the timing alignment as structural and deliberate. SpaceX remains privately held, so there is no directly investable US-listed equity that is the primary subject of this thesis.
detail: Baker observes and Johnsen confirms that Starlink V3 (enabled by Starship) generates incremental cash flows and unlocks direct-to-cell (new high-margin revenue) precisely when orbital compute requires heavy capital deployment. This sequencing reduces external financing risk and is described as intentional Elon-level capital planning.
confidence_evidence: Both parties affirm the timing alignment; CFO credits Elon's vision for the sequencing but does not provide specific cash flow figures.
quote_fragment: Starlink growth is cash flows — that unlocks direct to sell right when orbital compute needs capital
